coredata.dc:title: Tunable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from the biomass of spent tea leaves as supercapacitor electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Inayat, Abid
Albalawi, Karma
Rehman, Ata-ur
Adnan,
Saad, Aiman Y.
Saleh, Ebraheem Abdu Musad
Alamri, Mubarak A.
El-Zahhar, Adel A.
Haider, Ali
Abbas, Syed Mustansar
coredata.dc:description: A simple and cost-effective method has been used for obtaining highly conductive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal oxidation of spent tea leaves. The obtained CQDs are characterized by various analytical techniques demonstrating a crystalline structure. CQDs proved to be exceptional working electrodes in the three-electrode cell in H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Based on X-ray diffraction and microscopic analysis, the synthesized CQD is crystalline, with a diameter of 2–5 nm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry and charge-discharge measurements are used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of CQDs in an aqueous electrolyte. The CQD-based supercapacitor demonstrated a superior specific capacitance of 302.0 F g 1 at a current of 0.5 Ag 1, cyclability with 144.4 F g 1 at 20 A g 1 after 5000 cycles, and rate performance of 186.4 F g 1 at 20 A g 1. The CQDs electrode also exhibited an enhanced energy density and power density of 41.9 Wh g 1 and 250 W g 1, respectively at a current rate of 0.5 A g 1. In experiments, it is demonstrated that many thousands of cycles could be run consistently at near 100% efficiency and a stable capacity. This explains the possibility of using spent tea leaves as a bioresource, the ease of synthetically producing these materials, and the favourable redox reaction of CQDs for high-energy devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Carbon quantum dots
Biomass
Spent tea leaves
Energy density

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Adnan is thankful to the Higher Education Commission (HEC) of Pakistan for research support under the Local Challenge Fund (20-LCF-278/RGM/R&D/HEC/2020). Ali Haider and Syed Mustansar Abbas are thankful to HEC (20–14419/NRPU/R&D/HEC/2021) for financial support. The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through the Large Groups Project under grant number (2/128/43).

Adnan is thankful to the Higher Education Commission (HEC) of Pakistan for research support under the Local Challenge Fund (20-LCF-278/RGM/R&D/HEC/2020). Ali Haider and Syed Mustansar Abbas are thankful to HEC (20-14419/NRPU/R&D/HEC/2021) for financial support. The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through the Large Groups Project under grant number (2/128/43).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Powder XRD pattern and (b) FTIR profile of CQDs.
(a) Aqueous solution of as-synthesized CQDs, (b,c,d) morphology and crystalline structure of CQDs at different TEM resolutions with inset in (c) showing the HRTEM lattice spacing.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum of CQDs, (b) high-resolution C 1 s spectrum, (c) high-resolution N 1 s spectrum, (d) high-resolution O 1 s spectrum.
(a) Raman spectrum and (b) thermogram of CQDs.

(a) Electrochemical performance of CQDs in SCs, (a) CV curves of the CQD electrode with potential from 0.2–0.8 V at different scan rates (5–200 mV s 1) in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution, (b) GCD curves of the CQDs at different current densities (0.5–20 A g 1) in the 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution, (c) specific capacitance change of the CQD electrode as a function of the current density.

(a) cyclic stability of the CQD electrode at current-density values of 10, 15, and 20 A g−1 during 5000 cycles, (b) the Ragone plot of the CQD electrode at various current densities, and (c) the Nyquist plot for the CQD electrode; the inset displays the corresponding equivalent circuit along with an enlargement of the Nyquist plot in the high-frequency region.

(a) Comparison of specific capacitances at different current densities between the CQDs and the previously reported carbon-based electrodes using H2SO4 as an electrolyte, (*) indicates the ED and the PD at the specified current density.
Comparison of specific capacitance, ED and PD of CQDs electrode vs. reported SC carbon electrodes in H2SO4 electrolyte.

row.entry: Activated carbon derived from melaleuca bark

head.ce:title: Tunable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from the biomass of spent tea leaves as supercapacitor electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: i
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Department, National Centre for Physics, Islamabad, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience and Technology Department, National Centre for Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanoscience and Technology Department, National Centre for Physics, Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and cost-effective method has been used for obtaining highly conductive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal oxidation of spent tea leaves. The obtained CQDs are characterized by various analytical techniques demonstrating a crystalline structure. CQDs proved to be exceptional working electrodes in the three-electrode cell in H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. Based on X-ray diffraction and microscopic analysis, the synthesized CQD is crystalline, with a diameter of 2–5 nm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry and charge-discharge measurements are used to evaluate the electrochemical performance of CQDs in an aqueous electrolyte. The CQD-based supercapacitor demonstrated a superior specific capacitance of 302.0 F g 1 at a current of 0.5 Ag 1, cyclability with 144.4 F g 1 at 20 A g 1 after 5000 cycles, and rate performance of 186.4 F g 1 at 20 A g 1. The CQDs electrode also exhibited an enhanced energy density and power density of 41.9 Wh g 1 and 250 W g 1, respectively at a current rate of 0.5 A g 1. In experiments, it is demonstrated that many thousands of cycles could be run consistently at near 100% efficiency and a stable capacity. This explains the possibility of using spent tea leaves as a bioresource, the ease of synthetically producing these materials, and the favourable redox reaction of CQDs for high-energy devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analysed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pursuing a modernized urban lifestyle and increased population has led to a massive spike in energy demand. Therefore, there has been an unprecedented demand in recent years for high-performance energy storage devices. Among the best examples of traditional energy storage devices are batteries and EDL capacitors (double-layer). Electrostatic interactions of large specific surface area electrodes are the basis of EDL capacitors (supercapacitors) possessing very high-power density (PD) but their energy density (ED) usually falls well below the level of metal-ion batteries. Moreover, since they store energy using an electrostatic mechanism, they can have an excellent capacity for retention, show a very long cycle life, and can charge very quickly [1–3]. Therefore, it is of considerable research interest to further develop the valuable parameters of supercapacitors and hence make improvements in the ED of these devices. To improve the device performance of the supercapacitor, many different types of materials like metal oxides [4], conductive polymers [5], metal sulfides [6], graphene [7,8], carbon nanotubes [9], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [10], MOFs derived porous carbon [11], doped porous carbon [12], polyoxometallates [13], metal hydroxides [14], etc., have been explored. Among them, carbon-based materials have attracted extensive attention as supercapacitor electrodes owing to natural abundance, low cost, excellent physiochemical stability, and high conductivity. They generally show EDLC-type storage behaviour with robust ion adsorption/desorption kinetics which mainly depend on porous structure and specific surface area [15]. Recently, Pokharel et. al., [16] derived mesoporous carbon from MOFs demonstrating a capacitance of 384 and 303 F g 1 at 0.1 A g 1 and 10 A g 1, respectively. Altin et al., [17] developed a low-cost graphene ink-based printing device for supercapacitor application with a specific capacitance of 102 F g 1 and 22% capacity decay observed after 1000 cycles.

Researchers have expanded the range of applications for carbon materials due to the discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with tunable properties [2,18–20]. Compared to other nanomaterials, CQDs have excellent electrical and optical properties, higher water solubility, and high chemical stability [19]. Therefore, this material is being studied in various fields, such as electrocatalysis [21], photocatalysis [22], biosensing [23], gas sensing [24], and energy storage [3]. Few synthetic techniques have been developed to prepare CQDs, such as laser ablation of carbon targets [25,26], purification of soot from the arc discharge experiment [27], catalytically opening C60 cages on ruthenium [28], and plasma treatment of single-layer graphene [29]. CQDs have also been prepared by hydrothermally cutting graphene sheets [30], breakdown of polyethene glycol and saccharides using microwaves [31], acidic treatment of carbon fibres [32] and electrochemically oxidizing graphite rods [25,33]. The hydrothermal process is strongly preferred because it yields high-quality carbon materials that are environmentally friendly and quick to prepare [20,30,34,35]. Nowadays, the precursors for preparing CQDs can be found in a variety of carbon-rich compounds [32,36–38]. Recent work has demonstrated that bio-wastes can be used to prepare CQDs, providing an inexpensive precursor method [34,36,38,39]. The composition of bio-wastes with other elements has a wide range of applications. Biomass derived carbon materials are popular due to their low cost, stability, versatility, large specific surface area and good conductivity. The most widely used biomass sources include coconut shell [40], rice husk [41], soybean root [42] and sugarcane bagasse [43].

The spent tea leaves are rich bio-source and contain several organic compounds such as cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, condensed tannin [44], and many water-soluble proteins [45] which can serve as a useful carbon source for preparing various carbon-based materials. Therefore, spent tea can be utilized to derive carbon precursor as an electrode material for supercapacitor application [46–48]. In the year 2025, the production of tea is estimated to reach 8.52 million tonnes, making it a beverage of great consumption worldwide with an annual growth rate of 4.9%. The estimated consumption of black tea only in the US is about 87% while worldwide consumption is above 75–80% [49]. Tea is one of the cheapest beverages and is derived from Camellia sinensis L. Tea is classified into three types based on the degree of fermentation, black tea (fully fermented), oolong tea (semi-fermented) and green tea (unfermented) [50]. Spent tea leaves are generally discarded after usage and remain a challenge for solid waste management and treatment. The carbon-based compounds obtained after the decomposition of waste tea leaves contain a significant amount of N- and O functionalities resulting in inherently N-doped materials which is beneficial in improving electrical conductivity, capacity enhancement and surface wettability [51].

In contrast to previous experiments, we introduced a promising method for synthesizing CQDs by using the one-step hydrothermal process. The precursor in our investigation is the spent tea leaves as they are easily available, have low cost, and are easy to harvest. Tea is consumed widely worldwide, and the spent leaves are wasted. There are no reports on CQDs synthesizing using spent tea leaves and their utilization as the electrode for supercapacitors. The experiment provides a new understanding of how various outputs can be obtained from a single biomass precursor.

During the experiment, dried spent tea leaves were mixed with de-ionized water and ethanol (0.5/0.5 ratio) at a concentration of 50 mL. To perform the hydrothermal process, we placed this mixture in an autoclave made of stainless steel and held it at a temperature of 130 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, a dark brown suspension was obtained with few precipitations at its bottom. To obtain the CQDs solution, the membrane-filtered suspension was again filtered several times using a 0.2 µm syringe filter to separate the precipitates from the suspension. A rotary evaporator was then used to collect the consequently formed CQDs. Before their use, the CQDs were subjected to a four-day dialysis treatment to eliminate excess reaction ions.

A JEOL-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV is used to gather and analyze high-resolution TEM images. To prepare the samples for TEM analysis, the CQDs were dispersed in isopropanol and then vacuum dried at 25 °C overnight. The FTIR measurements were carried out to find the chemical groups attached to CQDs using FTIR spectrometer Vector-22 from Bruker. Utilizing a D8 powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm), the crystal structure, composition, and phase analysis of the CQDs powder was carried out. With a Renishaw Raman spectrometer, the spectra were acquired with 785 nm Raman excitation. XPS (Scienta-Omicron) was employed to analyze the surface features of the sample using a micro-focused monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. The binding energy of various elements was calculated by keeping the C 1 s peak (284.8 eV) as a calibration reference. To test the thermal stability of the sample, a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed. The temperature of the samples was raised at 10 °C min−1 using the TGA Q-500 device (TA Instruments) and the corresponding weight loss was measured.

A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3 mm was used as a substrate to prepare a working electrode (WE). Before loading any sample, the GCE was polished several times using a polishing cloth and repeatedly rinsed with de-ionized water during each step. Finally, the electrode was ultrasonicated in a mixture of DI water and ethanol and left to dry at room temperature. The preparation of the WE began with the making of a 10-µL CQDs dispersion having a concentration of 1.5 mg mL 1. The sample was cast onto the tip of GCE by carefully dropping a mixture of active material with 5% Nafion solution onto the electrode and then allowing it to dry at room temperature, thereby forming the sample adsorbed GCE. The same procedure was adopted for preparing working electrodes with different CQDs loadings that resulted in a smooth layer of the sample on the GCE electrode.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and charge-discharge measurements were evaluated using the VersaSTAT 4 Potentiostat Galvanostat instrument (Princeton Applied Research). We used a three-electrode setup consisting of as-prepared CQDs as WE, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference and the platinum wire as a counter electrode. The CV and GCD measurements were taken within a voltage window of − 0.2 and + 0.8 V versus the SCE at a series of sweep rates in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution, while the EIS experiments were conducted in the frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz at an open circuit potential (OCV).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the crystallinity of CQDs as shown in Fig. 1 a. The wide-angle powder XRD pattern demonstrates broad diffraction peaks centred at (25.7°) and 42.6°, which are assigned to the (002) and (100) planes of the hexagonal graphitic structure due to highly disordered carbon atoms present in CQDs. The d-spacing of the synthesized CQDs was calculated using Bragg’s equation, nλ= 2dsinθ where, λ is the wavelength of X-rays used, n is a positive integer, and θ is the peak position. The d-spacing of CQDs comes out to be 3.77 Å and 2.12 Å, respectively, which is larger than that for bulk graphitic structure (3.34 Å) [52]. Thus, CQDs may have a turbostratic carbon structure.

The as-synthesized CQDs have been subjected to FT-IR analysis to study their surface groups and structure. Fig. 1 b shows characteristic transmission bands associated with −OH and −CN stretching vibrations at around 3405 and 3229 cm−1 while the transmission signals at 2925 and 1591 cm−1 are related to the C−H and −NH2 stretching vibrations. Furthermore, signals occurring at 1728 and 1400 cm−1 are possibly caused by −COO stretching vibrations and -OH bending vibrations which are respectively asymmetric and symmetric [53]. Based on these findings, it is likely that the carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amine groups in CQDs originate from the biomass precursor used in the reaction. The CQDs surface is highly hydrophilic and have sufficient carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, as indicated by FTIR analysis. This property of the hydrophilic surface may explain its excellent dispersibility in water.

The TEM analysis carried out at different resolutions demonstrates the synthesis of CQDs in sufficient quantity with spherical morphology and crystalline structure. The synthesis adopted in this study does not involve any corrosive acid or base treatments and the unreacted precursor components are removed using dialytic treatment resulting in an environmentally friendly technique for synthesizing CQDs with high reproducibility. As shown in Fig. 2 c,d the CQDs are evenly dispersed with a size distribution of almost 2–5 nm and clear lattice fringes are visible with typical carbon d-spacing values.

The surface elemental composition was measured using the XPS technique as shown in Fig. 3 a-d. The survey scan showed the existence of O 1 s (531.3 eV), N 1 s (400.1 eV), and C 1 s (284.8 eV). The C 1 s (Fig. 3 b) is further deconvoluted into multiple peaks appearing at 284.3, 285.1, 286.4 and 288.3 eV and are related to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively. Fig. 3 c shows that nitrogen occurs as C–N and N–H bonds in the form of deconvoluted N 1 s peaks at 399.5 and 400.9 eV, respectively. The O 1 s signal is further resolved into two peaks that are attributed to CO and C-O existing at 531.2 and 532.6 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 d. The results suggest that the as-synthesized CQDs possess diverse polar groups in form of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amines [54]. A combination of these functional groups related to O and N enhanced the water solubility of the resulting CQDs.

Raman spectra of CQDs are recorded to further reveal the crystallinity of the sample. CQDs with broad features exhibit characteristic Raman disorder band (D) and graphitic band (G) located at 1369 and 1587 cm−1, respectively. The graphitic band is found to be more intense than the D-band as shown in Fig. 4 a indicating there are principally more carbons with sp2 character than hybrid carbons with sp3 character in CQDs [55]. The Raman spectra thus reveal that the CQDs are mostly made up of crystalline sp2 carbons with some defect-bearing sp3 carbons.

CQDs were studied in the air for thermal stability up to 1000 °C. The recorded thermogram is shown in Fig. 4 b. Approximately 14% of the initial weight loss occurs when the surface-adsorbed water molecules evaporate, and the decomposition of weakly bonded species between quantum-size particles starts [56]. While almost 8% of the secondary weight loss is due to the removal of crystalline and chemisorbed water molecules. Once the thermogram reaches 500 °C, there is a rapid, continuous loss of weight until about 750 °C, after which it becomes stable up to 1000 °C. Our study revealed an approximately 78% loss of sample weight at the third stage implying that the functional groups and the graphitic structure have been detached.

The CV curves of WE at scan rates varying from 5 to 200 mV s 1 are obtained using 1 M H2SO4 as an electrolyte and within a potential window from 0.2–0.8 V as shown in Fig. 5 a. An ideal supercapacitor CQD property is revealed from a nearly rectangular CV-curve shape. The varying sweep rate rendered a high peak current for the CQDs with a minimal change in the curve shape, signifying higher charge/discharge response reversibility, fast electronic and ionic transportations, and low ohmic resistance. A clear drop in the specific capacitance is observed at the higher sweep rates. Generally, the electrolytic diffusion between the inner active mass of the electrode and its outer surface occurs at the lower sweep rates; however, at the higher sweep rates, there is a delay in the diffusion phenomenon due to the adsorption of anions on the electrode surface, shutting off a sufficient active electrode mass from the electrode's redox activity [57]. It is believed that the maintenance of the CQDs with several charge carriers and superior conductivity with continuous electron transfer is possible. It is thought that the presence of the surface functionalities on CQDs supplements the specific capacitance by facilitating the redox reactions [58].

The charge-discharge measurements of the CQD electrode are obtained at current densities ranging from 0.5 to 20 A g 1 and are shown in Fig. 5 b. The specific capacitance from the charge-discharge measurements was calculated using Eq. (1), where the specific capacitance is indicated as Cs (F g 1), charge/discharge current as I (A), charge/discharge time Δt (s), the mass of the active electrode material as m (g), and voltage window as ΔV (V):
(1)
C
s
=
I ∆ t
m Δ V

The charge-discharge curves demonstrated a typical pseudocapacitive nature with a sharp potential-variation region followed by slow potential decay. At the same time, a negligible IR drop of the CQD electrode indicates a high conductivity. Fig. 5 c demonstrates a change in the specific capacitance as a function of current density. The GCD curves are reasonably symmetrical, indicating higher reversibility of the electrode reactions with a rapid electron transfer, which achieves fast charge gains/losses.

At a current density of 1 A g 1, the CQDs showed a high specific capacitance of 274.1 F g 1 however, the specific capacitance decreases as the current density is increased since it becomes difficult for the inner active sites to complete the redox processes at elevated current rates [56]. The capacity retention of ∼81.6% at 10 A g 1 demonstrates the superb electrochemical property of the as-prepared CQDs. To further investigate the cyclic stability of the CQD electrode, the charge-discharge tests are carried out for 5000 cycles at 10, 15, and 20 A g 1 and the results are shown in Fig. 6a. The specific-capacity retentions of 93.8%, 89.8%, and 77.4% are observed at the currents of 10, 15, and 20 A g 1, respectively. This high-capacity retention is due to the CQDs' excellent electronic and ionic conductivity and structural adaptability, preventing any mechanical degradation during a continuous insertion/extraction cycle.

The ED and the PD are two important parameters that need to be evaluated for the energy storage of a supercapacitor and are calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively, as follows:
(2)
E =
1
2
C
s
Δ
V
2
3.6
(3)
P =
3600 E
t
Where E is the ED, P is the PD, I is the charge-discharge current, discharge time is shown as Δt, while ΔV is the scanned-potential window, and the mass of the active electrode material is given as m. From the total active mass of the electrode material, the CQD-electrode Ragone plot (PD vs. ED) is demonstrated in Fig. 6b, wherein it is shown that the PD and the ED reached 25.8 W Kg 1 and 10,000 Wh Kg 1 at 20 A g 1, respectively. The high ED of the CQDs suggests their capabilities in meeting the requirements of large capacitance and extended cycle life.

To find the internal resistance of the electrode and its effect against the electrolyte, the Nyquist plot of the CQD electrode was measured from 105 kHz to 0.01 Hz and is illustrated in Fig. 6c. The plot was fitted using an equivalent circuit model that is given in the inset of Fig. 6c and comprises components like the constant phase element (CPE), ohmic resistance (Ro), Warburg resistance (Wo), and charge-transfer resistance (Rct). The lower frequency part shows linearity corresponding to the Wo, but at the region of higher frequency, an incomplete semicircle indicates Rct at the interface between electrode and electrolyte. The narrow semicircle points to low ion-diffusion resistance, while the low-frequency region is close to a vertical line, suggesting an almost-ideal CQD-electrode supercapacitive property. It can be inferred that the inherent conductive CQDs reduced the Rct, leading to higher ionic and electronic-conduction activities through the charge-transfer interactions. In addition to having a highly accessible electrochemical surface, the uniform structure of CQDs allows the full active sites to be utilized to maximize electrochemical performance.

The electrochemical results that were obtained from the CV and charge-discharge tests adequately demonstrate the promising potentiality of CQDs prepared through a green synthesis approach concerning a high-performance supercapacitor. As shown in Fig. 7 and Table 1, the electrochemical performance of the CQD electrode is much better than those of most of the earlier reports on C-based electrodes for which H2SO4 served as the electrolyte in the supercapacitor tests [59–67].

The spent tea leaves as a used biomass source have been used to synthesize CQDs in good yield following a one-step hydrothermal approach. The CQDs are prepared to have a crystalline structure with an average diameter of around 2–5 nm. The electrochemical capability of CQDs has been tested using a three-electrode assembly and the CQDs showed excellent stability as a supercapacitor electrode. The CQD-based supercapacitor at a current rate of 0.5 A g 1 exhibited a superior specific capacitance of 302.0 F g 1, and the specific-capacity retention values after 5000 cycles are 93.8%, 89.8%, and 77.4% at 10, 15, and 20 A g 1, respectively. Furthermore, an ED and a PD of 41.9 Wh kg 1 and 250 W kg 1, respectively, were achieved at a current rate of 0.5 A g 1. An excellent cycle over-cycle stability with high efficiency is also observed. Resulting from the presence of nano-sized CQDs, the active sites of particles were significantly improved, as were surface roughness and electrical conductivity further, the ion transfer and accessibility to electrolyte ions were enhanced. Developing new CQD-based electrode materials for energy storage could be a sustainable approach from the results of the present study. CQDs formed from spent tea leaves offer an abundance of possibilities for incorporating CQDs into composites with transition-metal oxides and sulfides to improve supercapacitor properties, and research in this area would lead to the development of multiple useful materials for supercapacitors.

Abid Inayat: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Karma Albalawi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ata-ur-Rehman: Resources, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Adnan: Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Aiman Y. Saad: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Ebraheem Abdu Musad Saleh: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Mubarak A. Alamri: Data curation, Formal analysis. Adel A. El-Zahhar: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Ali Haider: Funding acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Syed Mustansar Abbas: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Adnan is thankful to the Higher Education Commission (HEC) of Pakistan for research support under the Local Challenge Fund (20-LCF-278/RGM/R&D/HEC/2020). Ali Haider and Syed Mustansar Abbas are thankful to HEC (20-14419/NRPU/R&D/HEC/2021) for financial support. The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through the Large Groups Project under grant number (2/128/43).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A survey of hybrid energy devices based on supercapacitors
Green. Energy Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Gao, Z. Luo, C. Liu, S. Fan, A survey of hybrid energy devices based on supercapacitors, Green Energy & Environment. xx (2022) xx.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advancements in supercapacitors based on different electrode materials: Classifications, synthesis methods and comparative performance
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Lamba, P. Singh, P. Singh, P. Singh, Bharti, A. Kumar, M. Gupta, Y. Kumar, Recent advancements in supercapacitors based on different electrode materials: Classifications, synthesis methods and comparative performance, Journal of Energy Storage. 48 (2022) 103871.
